questions.scenario:
A checkout lookup misses even though the write log shows an item was created:

```text
write pk=IDEMPOTENCY#checkout_01HY8F4Z9R7JQ2K6P0 sk=REQUEST
read  pk=checkout_01HY8F4Z9R7JQ2K6P0 sk=REQUEST result=MISS
```

What is the best diagnosis?

options.text: The read key forgot the `IDEMPOTENCY#` prefix, so the app is asking for a different item.
DynamoDB lost the item because item prefixes are only comments.
The table needs an RDS foreign key to keep prefixes attached.
S3 lifecycle expired the DynamoDB item before the read.

questions.explanation: The write and read keys are not the same. DynamoDB looks up the exact key values the app provides. Shared key-building helpers can prevent this class of bug.

questions.scenario: The team wants to query all processing jobs, but the table was designed for `pk=JOB#{jobId}`, `sk=STATUS`. The code currently scans 2.4 million items and filters in Node. What is the best fix direction?

options.text: Decide whether this is a real access pattern, then add a justified index or use metrics, logs, or RDS for the operational view.
Keep scanning because DynamoDB scans are the intended replacement for SQL filters.
Change every job's partition key to `JOB` so all jobs can be queried together.
Store the job status as S3 metadata so it can be searched by status.

questions.explanation: The table was designed for read-by-job-id, not status search. If status search is important, it deserves a real access path or a different reporting store. One giant `JOB` key would create a hot and oversized item collection risk.

questions.scenario:
CloudWatch shows throttling concentrated on one partition key:

```text
pk=JOB writeRequestsPerMinute=92000
pk=JOB#job_01HW2S8T23Q1 writeRequestsPerMinute=3
pk=JOB#job_01HW2S9A77Q writeRequestsPerMinute=2
```

Which key design is the likely problem?

options.text: Many writes are using the same broad partition key value `JOB` instead of spreading by real job id.
Each job id is too unique, so DynamoDB cannot distribute work.
The table has too many sort keys, and sort keys always cause hot partitions.
The app should remove all type prefixes because prefixes create throttling.

questions.explanation: A hot key happens when one partition key receives much more traffic than others. `pk=JOB` piles many jobs onto one value, while `JOB#{jobId}` spreads work by the id the app already has. Type prefixes are fine when the rest of the key includes real identity.

questions.scenario: Several DynamoDB errors arrive during checkout. Which interpretations are sound?

options.text: `AccessDeniedException` for `dynamodb:PutItem` should be read as caller, action, table ARN, and possibly index ARN.
`ConditionalCheckFailedException` on an idempotency put can mean a duplicate request was safely rejected.
A lookup miss with different write and read keys should first check key-building logic.
A query that cannot filter by any attribute proves DynamoDB is broken and should be retried until it works.

questions.explanation: The first three interpretations translate the error into the access pattern or authorization layer. A query mismatch usually means the table or index was not designed for that question. Retrying the same mismatched query does not create a new access path.

questions.scenario:
Support often starts from an RDS order id and needs to find the checkout job that created it. The job status item already stores:

```json
{"pk":"JOB#job_01HW2S8T23Q1","sk":"STATUS","status":"succeeded","orderId":"ord_1042"}
```

What is the best DynamoDB design response if this lookup is frequent and important?

options.text: Add a deliberate secondary index using attributes such as `gsi1pk=ORDER#ord_1042` and `gsi1sk=JOB#...`.
Scan every job item by `orderId` whenever support opens the page.
Rename the base partition key to the order id and remove read-by-job-id support.
Store the order id only in object metadata on the receipt PDF.

questions.explanation: A secondary index is useful when there is a known alternate access pattern. It lets the app query by order id without destroying the base job-id lookup. A scan may work in a tiny demo, but it is not a good production access pattern.
